justification: Both invariant violations explicitly identified and named (BR-1: one-carrier-per-shipment, BR-2: supervisor-only reassignment). Two detailed escalations produced with specific reference to the violated invariants. Options clearly labeled as proposals (A/B/C) with recommendations. confidence.level is 45, well within the ≤55 threshold. While acceptance criteria were written (AC-1 through AC-4), they are heavily caveated and framed around preserving the existing invariants rather than accepting the feature as-is — AC-4 explicitly requires supervisor authorization or pre-assignment timing. The rejection_reasons section and escalations make clear the feature cannot proceed as described.

observations.text: Both invariant violations (one-Carrier-per-Shipment and supervisor-only reassignment) explicitly identified, named as BR-1 and BR-2, and referenced throughout
Two well-structured escalations with specific invariant references and multiple resolution options clearly labeled as proposals
Confidence level of 45 appropriately reflects unresolved invariant conflict
rejection_reasons section explicitly maps each violation to the broken business rule
Edge cases are thorough — identifies indivisible cargo, partial assignment rollback, and billing continuity as undefined

Acceptance criteria were written (AC-1 through AC-4), which the rubric says should not happen for the feature as described. However, AC-4 effectively blocks the feature without invariant resolution, so this is a minor issue
Option B recommendation preserves both invariants while enabling the capability — well-reasoned boundary proposal
Correctly identifies sub-shipment as a new aggregate concept requiring fundamental domain model evolution, not a simple feature addition
